blurb: Upright candlestick spikes of starry flowers (lead form: crisp white 'Icicle', though blue and pink cultivars abound) rise above a neat, clumping base in summer — a tidy, heat-tolerant pollinator favorite that rebloms freely with deadheading.
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
spread: Clumping; expands slowly by short rhizomes; non-invasive
seasons: Rosette of dark green leaves from spring; dense flower spikes Jun–Aug with deadheading; foliage remains tidy through fall; dies to ground in winter
wildlife: Strong pollinator draw — bumblebees, honeybees, small native bees, and butterflies work the spikes; a good front-of-border bee plant
origin: Europe to northern and central Asia (widespread)
habitat: Dry grasslands, rocky slopes, and open woodland margins

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3–8 — extremely cold-hardy and fully reliable across the Front Range (zones 5b–6a). Emerges early in spring and withstands late frosts without damage. No special winter protection needed; the clump dies to the ground and re-emerges strongly each spring.[1]
care.planting: Plant in spring (April–May) after the last frost date or in early fall (August–mid-September) on the Front Range. Spring planting is most common — transplants establish quickly in warming soil before summer heat. Fall planting works if done six or more weeks before a hard freeze so roots can anchor. Set the crown at soil level; do not bury it deeply. In containers (minimum 10–12 in. diameter), spike speedwell makes a tidy, upright accent. Container roots are more exposed to freezing than in-ground crowns — move pots to an unheated but frost-protected space (garage, shed) by late October, or sink the pot to its rim in a sheltered garden bed with mulch over it; do not leave a planted container on an open patio through a Boulder winter.[1]
care.sun: Full sun — 6 or more hours of direct sun daily gives the best, stiffest spikes and heaviest bloom. Tolerates light afternoon shade on the hottest Front Range sites, but flowering is reduced. Colorado's intense high-altitude sun is well-suited to this plant.[1]
care.soil: Well-drained, moderately fertile soil. Tolerates the alkaline clay-loam soils typical of Boulder yards without special amendment; in heavy clay, mixing in coarse grit or compost improves drainage and establishment. Does not demand rich soil — average garden soil is ideal. The one firm requirement: no standing water, especially in winter.[1]
care.water: Low to moderate. Once established (end of the first growing season), drought-tolerant and manageable on Front Range rainfall plus deep irrigation every 1–2 weeks in summer. Young transplants need consistent moisture the first season. Avoid overwatering; wet feet in winter are more damaging than summer drought.[1]
care.spacing: Space 12–18 in. apart; clumps reach 12–18 in. wide at maturity. For a border mass, plant at 12 in. for quicker fill-in. The upright habit combines well with mounding perennials placed in front.[1]
care.bloom: June through August on the Front Range, with deadheading extending the season into September. Flower spikes are densely packed with small, starry blooms: crisp white in 'Icicle' (the lead form recommended here), vivid blue in cultivars like 'Royal Candles', and pink in 'Rotfuchs'. Each spike opens from the bottom up; cut spikes off at a lateral leaf or bud as they finish to promote side-branch spikes.[1]
care.feeding: Light feeder. A top-dress of balanced slow-release fertilizer or compost in early spring supports strong bloom. Avoid excess nitrogen, which drives leafy growth at the expense of spikes and makes stems floppy.[1]
care.maintenance: Deadhead spent spikes promptly — cut back to a lateral leaf or shoot — to encourage a second and sometimes third flush of bloom through summer. After all bloom is finished in late summer, cut stems back to the basal rosette. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in early spring or after bloom in late summer to maintain vigor and flowering quality; old clumps can become congested and bloom less.[1]
care.troubles: Generally trouble-free on the Front Range. Powdery mildew can appear in humid, overcrowded, or shaded conditions — space plants for airflow and avoid overhead watering at night. Root rot in poorly drained soil is the other main risk. Deer seldom browse it. No serious pest problems.[1]
care.companions: Bloody cranesbill, catmint, salvia, and cushion spurge make excellent partners in a pollinator-friendly, low-water border. The vertical spike habit contrasts well with mounding plants like hardy geranium or ornamental grasses. White-form 'Icicle' is particularly effective paired with blue salvias or lavender.[1]
care.propagation: Division is the easiest and most reliable method: divide clumps every 3–4 years in early spring (before growth begins) or in late summer after bloom, splitting the crown into sections each with shoots and roots, and replanting immediately at 12–18 in. spacing. Spring division is preferred on the Front Range. Basal stem cuttings taken in spring (3–4 in. non-flowering shoots with a heel, stuck in moist perlite) root in 2–3 weeks and are useful for scaling up a named cultivar. Seed germinates readily at 65–70°F without stratification — sow indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct-sow in spring; seedlings typically bloom in their second year. Named cultivars may not come true from seed and are best divided or cutting-propagated.[1]

edible.summary: Spike speedwell is a low-toxicity ornamental with no established food use — not poisonous, but not a food plant.[1][2]
edible.caution: Veronica spicata has no recognized culinary use and should not be eaten. The Veronica (speedwell) genus is broadly non-toxic and not flagged as poisonous, and a few relatives (e.g. V. officinalis) have a folk herbal-tea history — but this border ornamental and its named cultivars ('Icicle', 'Royal Candles') are not documented food or medicinal plants, so be conservative and do not consume it. As with any non-food ornamental, eating plant material in quantity may cause mild stomach upset.[1][2]
